title: Big Relief! CBDT Extends Form 10AB Filing Deadline: A Lifeline for 80G Renewals
content:
# Big Relief! CBDT Extends Form 10AB Filing Deadline: A Lifeline for 80G Renewals

As tax professionals, we've all seen trusts and institutions grappling with the stringent compliance requirements for obtaining and renewing their tax exemptions and Section 80G approvals. The consequences of missing a deadline can be severe, leading to loss of crucial tax benefits for the organization and, more importantly, for their donors. The continuous inflow of amendments and changes, especially around Form 10AB filings, has kept many on their toes, often leading to genuine hardships.

In what comes as a much-needed sigh of relief for the non-profit sector, the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has once again shown its pragmatic side. Through a significant **Circular No. 06/2026-Income Tax, dated July 02, 2026**, the CBDT has condoned the delay in electronically filing **Form 10AB** for the renewal of Section 80G registrations. This is a big one, as it addresses a persistent concern for many organizations.

### The Background: Why Form 10AB and Section 80G Matter

First, let's quickly recap. Under the Income-tax Act, 1961, charitable trusts, institutions, and religious bodies can claim tax exemption on their income under various sections, prominently **Section 12AB**. Equally vital is the approval under **Section 80G**, which allows donors to claim a deduction for their contributions to these approved entities. This dual benefit is the backbone of financial sustainability for countless non-profits.

The Finance Act, 2020, brought a paradigm shift, moving away from perpetual registrations to a time-bound system. Now, every existing approval under Section 80G (and 12A/12AB) needs to be re-registered or renewed periodically by filing **Form 10AB**. Typically, the application for renewal must be filed at least six months prior to the expiry of the existing registration. For many organizations whose existing approvals were set to expire on March 31, 2026, the statutory due date for filing Form 10AB was September 30, 2025.

We all know how challenging it can be to navigate new compliance landscapes and meet strict deadlines. Technical glitches on the portal, administrative oversights, or simply a lack of awareness can lead to missed deadlines. When Form 10AB is not filed on time, the consequences are dire: loss of tax-exempt status and, crucially, the inability for donors to claim deductions under Section 80G. This can put a huge dent in an organization's fundraising efforts, "badi mushkil ho jaati hai, isn't it?"

### The Timely Intervention: CBDT's Condonation Circular

Recognizing these genuine difficulties, the CBDT has stepped in, exercising its powers under **Section 119(2)(b)** of the Income-tax Act, 1961. This section empowers the Board to issue orders for condonation of delays in filing various applications or claims to mitigate genuine hardship.

The freshly issued Circular No. 06/2026, dated July 02, 2026, provides specific relief for the delayed filing of Form 10AB for **80G renewal**. Here’s the lowdown:

*   **Who Benefits?** This condonation is specifically for funds and institutions whose approval under Section 80G was expiring on March 31, 2026, and who could not furnish Form 10AB by the original due date of September 30, 2025, due to "bona fide reasons resulting in genuine hardship".
*   **The Window of Relief:** The CBDT has effectively condoned the delay for all such cases where Form 10AB was filed electronically during the period **October 01, 2025, to March 31, 2026**. This means that if you missed the September 30, 2025, deadline but managed to file the form by March 31, 2026, your delay is now officially condoned! "Yeh toh kaafi logon ke liye achhi khabar hai!"
*   **Reconsideration of Rejected Applications:** Here's another critical point: applications that were already rejected solely because they were filed beyond the September 30, 2025, deadline, but were filed within the condoned period (October 01, 2025, to March 31, 2026), will now be **deemed to have their delay condoned**. These applications will be re-examined on their merits by the jurisdictional Principal Commissioner of Income-tax (PCIT) or Commissioner of Income-tax (CIT).
*   **New Deadline for Disposal:** The jurisdictional PCIT/CITs have been authorized to dispose of these applications (including the previously rejected ones) on merits, and pass an order **on or before December 31, 2026**. This provides a clear timeframe for resolution.

### Important Clarification: No Automatic Approval!

While this circular offers a lifeline by condoning procedural delays, it is crucial to understand that it **does not confer automatic approval** under Section 80G(5). The applications will still undergo a thorough examination on their merits by the tax authorities. This means ensuring all other statutory requirements are met, and the genuineness of the organization's activities is beyond doubt.

### What This Means for Charitable Organizations

This CBDT circular is a testament to the Board's understanding of the practical challenges faced by the non-profit sector. It provides an invaluable opportunity for organizations that missed the initial tight deadline to regularize their Section 80G approvals. For many, this translates to:

*   **Continued Eligibility:** The ability to continue receiving donations for which donors can claim tax deductions.
*   **Reduced Compliance Risk:** Avoiding the significant penalties and loss of tax-exempt status that would have otherwise occurred.
*   **Operational Stability:** Maintaining financial viability and focusing on their charitable objectives without the added stress of a lapsed registration.

As professionals, our role is now to help these organizations understand the implications of this circular, ensure their applications (if already filed within the condoned period) are tracked, and assist those whose applications were previously rejected due to delay, to prepare for a re-assessment on merits.

The CBDT's decision reflects a balanced approach: enforcing compliance while also providing relief in cases of genuine hardship. It's a reminder that even in the complex world of tax laws, a human touch and practical understanding can make all the difference.

## Read the Official Document
*   [Download the Official Notification/Circular here](https://www.incometax.gov.in)

*Published on: 6 July 2026*
